title: It's War! (Part 2)
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: It’s War! - Pastor Tito Lyro - Ephesians 6_14.txt

outline.title: Paul's Military Imagery for the Christian Life
subpoints: Paul uses military imagery throughout his letters: fellow servants in Christ are called soldiers (Philippians 2:25; 2 Timothy 2:3), the Christian life is the good fight of faith (1 Timothy 6:12)
The longest military analogy in Paul's writing is the whole armor of God in Ephesians 6
Paul likely developed this analogy while living in house arrest in Rome, chained to a Roman soldier 24 hours a day — he could study the armor at length
The Roman soldier's armor was one of the most sophisticated defensive and offensive systems ever developed in the ancient world
Last week we established: the war is real, the enemy is powerful, and we fight from the victory of Christ — now we examine the first two pieces of armor

outline.title: The Belt of Truth: Girding Your Waist
subpoints: The Roman soldier's belt was the foundation piece — everything else was attached to it; without it, the other pieces could not function properly
Truth here is objective truth — the truth of God, the truth of Scripture, the truth of the gospel, the truth of who Christ is
Satan is the father of lies (John 8:44); his primary weapon is deception — therefore the Christian's primary defense is truth
Isaiah 11:5 describes the Messiah: 'Righteousness shall be the belt of his loins, and faithfulness the belt of his waist' — the Christian wears what the Messiah wore
Subjectively, truth means living with personal integrity — Psalm 51:6: 'You desire truth in the inward parts' — inner truthfulness is essential armor

outline.title: The Breastplate of Righteousness: Protecting the Heart
subpoints: The Roman breastplate protected the vital organs — heart and lungs; without it, a single blow was fatal
The righteousness here is primarily the imputed righteousness of Christ — the justification that covers us before God's judgment; no accusation can penetrate it
Isaiah 59:17 describes God himself putting on righteousness as a breastplate — the Christian's armor comes from God, not from his own moral achievement
Practically, this righteousness also means righteous living — a life of integrity that makes our enemies' accusations fall to the ground for lack of evidence
Daniel's enemies could find no fault in him except in the matter of his God — their only accusation was that he was more faithful to God than to the king

outline.title: Putting On Truth and Righteousness Today
subpoints: We are called to fight spiritual battles not primarily by external action but by the internal wearing of these pieces of armor
We gird ourselves with truth by knowing and meditating on Scripture, by refusing the lies of the enemy, and by living with personal integrity
We put on the breastplate of righteousness by resting in the justification Christ has secured, and by pursuing holy living that backs up our profession
Together these two pieces protect us at the foundation (belt) and at the heart (breastplate) — truth and righteousness are the core of the Christian's defense

summary:
Continuing from the previous week's introduction to the whole armor of God, Pastor Tito Lyro focuses on Ephesians 6:14 and the first two pieces of armor: the belt of truth and the breastplate of righteousness. The sermon begins by noting that Paul used military imagery throughout his letters, and that the Ephesians 6 passage is his most extended military analogy — likely developed while he was in house arrest in Rome, chained to a Roman soldier day and night.

The belt of truth is treated as the foundation piece: the Roman soldier's belt held everything else together; without it the other armor would not function. Truth here is both objective (the truth of God, Scripture, and the gospel — the opposite of Satan's lies in John 8:44) and subjective (personal integrity — Psalm 51:6). Isaiah 11:5 connects this armor to the Messiah himself, who girds his loins with righteousness and faithfulness. The Christian wears what Christ wore.

The breastplate of righteousness protects the vital organs — heart and lungs. The sermon develops this primarily in terms of imputed righteousness: the justification Christ has secured before God's judgment cannot be penetrated by any accusation. But it also means righteous living — a life of integrity that refuses to give the enemy legitimate grounds for accusation. Daniel in Daniel 6 is the illustration: his enemies searched for fault and could find none except that he was more faithful to God than to the king. That must be the church's condition: truth and righteousness so deeply worn that only faithfulness to God remains as an accusation.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Ephesians 6:10-20

Please remain standing for the reading of God's Word. We're going to turn to Ephesians chapter 6, the sixth chapter of the Book of Ephesians. We're going to read verses 10 through 20. We're going to remain in Ephesians 6 for a little bit of time. Next week we're going to turn to Romans 6 for the consideration of the resurrection, and then we'll return to Ephesians 6. This morning we're going to be considering verse 14 specifically, but we're going to read verses 10 through 20. This is the Word of our Lord.

*Finally, my brethren, be strong in the Lord and in the power of His might. Put on the whole armor of God, that you may be able to stand against the wiles of the devil. For we do not wrestle against flesh and blood, but against principalities, against powers, against the rulers of the darkness of this age, against spiritual hosts of wickedness in the heavenly places. Therefore take up the whole armor of God, that you may be able to stand in the evil day, and having done all, to stand. Stand therefore, having girded your waist with truth, having put on the breastplate of righteousness, and having shod your feet with the preparation of the gospel of peace; above all, taking the shield of faith with which you will be able to quench all the fiery darts of the wicked one. And take the helmet of salvation, and the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God; praying always with all prayer and supplication in the Spirit, being watchful to this end with all perseverance and supplication for all the saints — and for me, that utterance may be given to me, that I may open my mouth boldly to make known the mystery of the gospel, for which I am an ambassador in chains; that in it I may speak boldly, as I ought to speak.*

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray. Father, we pray that Your Spirit, through this passage, will speak to us today. We ask in Jesus' name. Amen. Please be seated.

## Paul's Military Imagery

In his writings, Paul often uses military imagery to illustrate the Christian life. For example, in Philippians 2 and in 2 Timothy he describes his fellow servants in Christ as soldiers. In 2 Timothy he says that the attitude of a Christian is one of a soldier who is engaged in warfare to the point that he doesn't even think about the trivial things of life. In 1 Timothy 6 he says that life is the good fight of faith — in the sense of a military engagement.

So here we have Paul often using the imagery of the military, of the soldier, of war, to illustrate the life of a Christian. The passage this morning includes the longest analogy of the Christian life as war and the passage that we are so familiar with — the whole armor of God.

And perhaps Paul got the idea for this analogy by looking at the Roman soldier who was attached to him twenty-four hours a day, seven days a week, as he sat in house arrest in Rome. The way they conducted imprisonment at this time was to have a soldier literally in chains with you, attached to you, every moment of the day and night. And Paul, with his brilliant theological mind, looked at this soldier who was attached to him and saw an analogy for the Christian life.

The Roman soldier's armor was one of the most sophisticated defensive and offensive weapons systems that had ever been developed in the ancient world up to that point. And Paul takes what he sees and applies it to what the church needs in order to fight the war that God has called us to fight.

## Where We Left Off

Last week we established several things. We established that the whole of the book of Ephesians is building toward this passage. We established that the Christian life is a life of spiritual war. We established that in order to fight, we need to be strong in the Lord — strong in the power of the Holy Spirit that already dwells in us, and strong through union with Christ who has already defeated our enemies.

We also established who the enemy is: not flesh and blood, but principalities, powers, rulers of the darkness of this age, and spiritual hosts of wickedness in the heavenly places. Satan is powerful, deceptive, and relentless. But he is a defeated foe — Christ defeated him at the cross and resurrection. And we fight from that victory.

This morning we are going to look at the first two pieces of armor in verse 14: the belt of truth and the breastplate of righteousness.

## The Belt of Truth: Your Foundation

Look at verse 14: *Stand therefore, having girded your waist with truth.*

In Roman military equipment, the belt was the foundation piece. Everything else was attached to the belt. The breastplate hung from the belt. The scabbard for the sword hung from the belt. The skirt of leather strips that protected the upper legs hung from the belt. Without the belt, the other pieces of armor could not function properly — they would fall or shift or impede movement. The belt held everything together.

So Paul says: the first thing you must put on is truth. And what is truth here?

First, it is objective truth — the truth of God, the truth of Scripture, the truth of who Christ is, the truth of the gospel. Jesus says in John 14:6: *I am the way, the truth, and the life.* Jesus is truth incarnate. And Satan — who is the father of lies (John 8:44), in whom there is no truth — attacks primarily through deception. His primary weapon is not brute force but lies.

Think of his strategy in the garden. He didn't overpower Adam and Eve. He lied. He deceived. He told Eve: *You will not surely die.* He questioned God's word. He questioned God's character. He told her: *God knows that when you eat of it your eyes will be opened, and you will be like God.* This is how he attacks: through lies, through distortion, through deception. And the defense against lies is truth.

Isaiah 11:5 describes the Messiah: *Righteousness shall be the belt of his loins, and faithfulness the belt of his waist.* So the armor that the Christian wears is the armor that the Messiah himself wears. We are dressed in what Christ is dressed in. And that begins with truth.

But there is also a subjective dimension to the belt of truth. It's not only knowing objective truth — it's living with personal integrity. Psalm 51:6 says: *Behold, You desire truth in the inward parts, and in the hidden part You will make me to know wisdom.* God desires truth in the inward parts — in the hidden places of the heart, where no one else can see. Inner truthfulness. No pretending. No performance. No deception of others or of yourself.

A Christian who is girded with truth is not only someone who knows the truth of the gospel but someone who lives with that truth at the center of everything — whose inner life corresponds to the outer confession.

And when you are girded with truth, Satan's lies cannot easily take hold. You know the truth of who God is. You know the truth of who you are in Christ. You know the truth about what this world is and what it is not. And his deceptions bounce off.

## The Breastplate of Righteousness: Protecting Your Heart

Look at the next phrase in verse 14: *having put on the breastplate of righteousness.*

The Roman breastplate — the thorax — was a piece of heavy metal armor that covered the torso from neck to waist, front and back. It protected the vital organs: the heart and the lungs. Without the breastplate, a single spear thrust or sword blow to the chest was fatal. The breastplate was not optional armor.

And Paul says: put on the breastplate of righteousness. Now what is this righteousness?

Primarily, it is the imputed righteousness of Christ — the righteousness that God gives to those who believe in Jesus. When you trust in Christ for salvation, God declares you righteous. He credits to your account the perfect obedience of Christ. And Satan — who is the accuser of the brethren — cannot successfully bring a charge against someone who is clothed in the righteousness of Christ. Every accusation he makes bounces off. Who shall bring a charge against God's elect? It is God who justifies.

Isaiah 59:17 describes God himself putting on righteousness as a breastplate: *For He put on righteousness as a breastplate, and a helmet of salvation on His head.* God's own armor — given to us. The Christian's breastplate of righteousness comes from God, not from his own moral achievement. We don't earn the breastplate by being good enough.

But there is also a practical dimension. The breastplate of righteousness also means living righteously — a life of integrity and holy living. And why does this matter for spiritual warfare?

Because if we are not living righteously, we give the enemy ammunition. When the enemy accuses us, he has grounds. When Satan whispers to a brother that he is a hypocrite, and it's true — the accusation has some force. When he tells the world that Christians are liars and frauds, and our lives confirm it — the breastplate has been removed.

But when our lives are marked by righteousness — when we live with integrity in the small things, when we are honest in our dealings, when we are pure in our relationships, when we walk the talk — the enemy's accusations fall to the ground for lack of evidence.

The great example of this is Daniel in Daniel 6. Daniel was serving as a high official under a foreign king, and his enemies wanted to bring him down. They searched for fault in his administration — and they could find nothing. They had to admit to one another: we will not find any charge against this Daniel unless we find it concerning the law of his God. That was the only accusation that stuck: he is too faithful in his religion. He is more faithful to God than he is to you, O King. Therefore we should throw him in the den of lions.

That must be us. More than ever, we must live righteous lives. Not perfect lives — but lives that demonstrate the truth of the Scriptures, including daily repentance and humility. So that when the enemy accuses us, the only accusation he can successfully make is that we are faithful to God.

Truth and righteousness — are you going to stand against Satan? Are you going to stand against his armies? Gird yourself with the truth of God. And put on the breastplate of righteousness that will protect your heart. Be ready to fight. Put on truth. Put on righteousness.

Let us pray together. Father in heaven, thank you that you are a God who reveals Yourself to us and has equipped us as well. We pray that we would faithfully follow Your call. We pray that Your Spirit, with what we'll set here this morning, will cause us to grow closer to You. We ask in Jesus' name. Amen.
